speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with John Eikum took place at his Lewiston home on December 8, 1975. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm going to ask you, what year were you born?

speaker: John Eikum
words: I was born in 1888.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you born here or back in the old country?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Back in Norway. I was born in Norway, October 28, 1888.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you when folks came over here?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, I lacked three months of being five years. We came to Tennessee in 1893, the fifth day of August.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember anything about that trip over here from Norway?

speaker: John Eikum
words: I was four and a half years old. I remember coming to Montana. My mother bought a Kern pie, and she opened it up and looked for it, to see what was inside of it, and she just threw it out the car window.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Then she saw some Indians outside along the platform, and she got so scared we just hid under the seats in the train. We heard so many Indian stories about the atrocities that the Indians...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your mother hide too, or just hid the kids?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Hid the kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Tell me, why did she throw the pie out the window?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, she didn't know what it was.

speaker: John Eikum
words: She said she wasn't about to eat anything like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how your parents happened to wind up going to Genesee instead of someplace else?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, yes. You see, there was four or five bachelors from the same place in Norway as my folks, and they came over here before, and they wrote back what nice country it was out here. Dad came over first. He came over in April of 1893, and then he sent back and brought the family over. My mother had quite a time on the trip. She got one of her sisters and another girl that was going to Alma, Wisconsin, and they both got to help care for the babies, you know. She had a four -month -old baby to take care of, and they got so seasick that they couldn't do a thing. So she had four children and her sister and this other girl to take care of. She didn't get seasick at all, so I guess that was a pretty good thing.

speaker: John Eikum
words: On August 5th, we landed in Genesee.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father have a place ready for you, a home?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, he had bought 80 acres, but then he had been hired out to warn the neighbors up there. So it was November before we moved into the place that he bought. That was after a wet year, you know, when they couldn't get their harvesting done.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Where did you live in the meantime, while you waited for the house to be... Well, we lived at the family where my dad was working. I see. She had a baby about that time, and she had a baby the first part of October, so mother took care of the baby.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And he was such a... He cried a lot, you know, and mother fixed that. She mashed potatoes and butter together. She just fed that to two -week -old baby all the potatoes and butter he could eat. Then he seemed satisfied. And he's still living up there. He's 82 years old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about that wet harvest? That year of the wet harvest? What did they tell you about that?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, I remember it. I remember when we moved over to the 80 acres that dad had bought. It's a driving rain. We were just as wet as drowned rats when we got over to the place. It was over a mile and a half, but you know, traveling in those days was pretty slow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have a wagon to travel in?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah. Bought a wagon and three head of horses from one of the neighbors.

speaker: John Eikum
words: That's all the start.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, in those days, the neighbors were all close together because they had 80 acres apiece all down the valley, you know. That made it pretty thickly populated for a while. Now when you go back in the valley, you don't see but half of the buildings that was there at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened during this 1893 wet harvest? Did many people go under in that?

speaker: John Eikum
words: They all went under. All went to two or three. Then they moved out. In 1895, they opened the Desper's Indian Reservation for settlement. Some of them went there and lots of them went up to Troy. Settled around Troy.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, it was pretty hard times. No money. They couldn't pay their debts.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And there was no chance of borrowing any money in those days. See, Dad, when he bought 80 acres, and he had to go clear to Colfax to get a loan to the Vermont Loan Company. And the same way when he paid it off, he had to go clear to Colfax.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I remember the time he went to Colfax to pay off the debt. Of course, he didn't pay much for it. He paid $700 for the 80 acres.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He paid the whole debt off at once?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, not all at once. But the final payment, he had to go to Colfax. To the Vermont Trust and Loan Company. They had offices in Colfax.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who got the land when people went under in that depression?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, John P. Waldman got the most of it. And the exchange bank of Genesee got the 400 acres I talked about the other day, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which 400?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Just below the church. At that time, it belonged to Sam Johnson. He went broke. He owed $400 at the store. And the bank took over, you see. They took the 400 acres for the $400 store bill. They moved up, over on the prairie.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Things were that bad that Sam Johnson couldn't manage to come up with $400?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, they didn't save a bushel of their grain that fall, you know.

speaker: John Eikum
words: It just stood in the field and rotted. Couldn't get no harvesting done. In those days, they all had spring grain. There was no such a thing as fall grain, fall seeded grain. And that made the harvest off a lake.

speaker: John Eikum
words: See, the first harvesting they tried to do was about the 10th day of September. That's when the wet weather started. And it rained continually for six weeks.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And they had a few steam thrashers, but the ground was wet. They couldn't move. I remember over there where my folks stayed. They got mired down on top of the hill. They weren't there for a pretty near a week to get the tractor loose, steam engines. They got mired right down, you know.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And I remember in 1894, I remember the first harvest that we had. We had some boats down on the flats. And we got our next door neighbor to cut that with a reaper.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you ever seen one of them?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've seen pictures of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The old reapers?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Just pictures. They had no drapers. They had a platform. And then they had an arm with four sweeps on. And that would come down and it would sweep off the grain and leave it loose here. It didn't tie it.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Then, of course, you had to tie it by hand. I remember Mother and I went out and tied it by hand. Every time the sweep would come around, they had four sweeps, so they would just sweep it off, you know, and leave it on the ground. Leave the bunches on the ground. Instead of tying it in bundles, they just left it loose.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much land did he have planted in oats that year?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Oh, about 15 acres.

speaker: John Eikum
words: 15 acres of oats down on the flat.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And then the way they harvested... Well, I guess I'm getting a little bit ahead of the way they farmed. It was all foot burners or walking plows. Three head of horses and a plow.

speaker: John Eikum
words: But the farms weren't very big. And there was no summer fall either in those days. It was all spring seeded.

speaker: John Eikum
words: But after a while, the wild oats got too powerful with wild oats. They helped the summer fall in order to kill the wild oats. You should have seen some of the fields at harvest time. Because when they cut for hay, they cut out the worst patches, you know, wild oats. Used that for hay. They had a great big patch here and there all over the field. Cut out for the wild oats.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did they just keep getting worse as the years went on?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Sure. Well, you see, they used headers. You had to wait until their grain was dead ripe, like we called it. It had to be dry. And then the wild oats got ahead of the wheat and the wild oats would all shatter out and cover the ground again. I picked up a clod when I was plowing in the next spring. I picked up a clod about that big. And there was 50 wild oats in that little clod. Now it got so bad, then they got the binders. Then you cut the grain before it was entirely ripe, you know. So you got lots of wild oats out of there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did they start summer fallowing? About what year?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Oh, around the year 1900 there. It was in the 1900s before they started summer fallowing.

speaker: John Eikum
words: The way Dad worked it, well, at that time in 1901 he bought an extra 40, so he had 120. Of course, he could have bought 80 acres three years before and he could have got the 80 acres for 1 ,300. No, he says he didn't want it at any price. Three years later he bought back half of it, 40 acres, and paid 1 ,600 for the 40 acres.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The land was getting that much more valuable that fast?

speaker: John Eikum
words: But it didn't compare to the price now, you know. They sold the poorest ranch up there, the Ducker's Head of the Poor's. Sold the other day for $1 ,300 an acre.

speaker: John Eikum
words: But he owed half of the country. Of course, that was, that bordered the Genesee Valley, but it was also what they called the German settlement. See, when they, the way they settled that country, there's a valley that runs up from Genesee along the, well, you come down there now, the main highway down there from Moscow and down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, where the valley runs along the creek?

speaker: John Eikum
words: That's Thorn Creek. That was a German settlement. And the Cow Creek, or the Genesee Valley, was all settled by Norwegians. And when you got over about a mile and a half further east, it was a little potlatch. That was all settled by Irishmen.

speaker: John Eikum
words: So they had three settlements there, the Irish and the Scandinavians and the Irish.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When they first started farming on land there, they didn't have to do too much clearing, did they?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No clearing at all, no timber at all. It's all open prairie in Genesee Valley. Of course, over in the land mill country where the Irishmen settled down on a little potlatch, that was scattered timber. Well, it was mostly, well, mostly, what we called, well, pine.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And the German settlement, that was open prairie, too, until you got pretty close to Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was growing on that originally? Was it bunch grass?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Bunch grass. Bunch grass.

speaker: John Eikum
words: You know, two sections out of every township was school land. We called that school section. Two sections. Section 16 and Section 36.

speaker: John Eikum
words: That was open country. I remember that was open country until 1901.

speaker: John Eikum
words: My, I had two uncles, they bought 80 acres apiece off that school land when it was open for sale. See, the money derived from the school section and went to the school district.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, this bunch grass, did you still have to run a plow through it to, did it take much work to get it ready to plant?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, you just put a plow to it. You had what they called a breaking plow.

speaker: John Eikum
words: That was, it was awful rich land, though. Of course, in those days, they raised all their crops without fertilizers of any kind.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And they raised, if they got 50 bushels, they thought they had a bumper crop, but the average was about 35 bushels to the acre.

speaker: John Eikum
words: But, they had a different method of farming. You plowed it and scattered the seed right at that broadcast seeder, most of them. And bought broadcast right in there and then just harrowed it. That's all they done to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You broadcast by hand?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No. No, we had a seven foot. Seven foot seeder, or they called it drill, but this time, they had tubes go down there and then they hit down and let it scatter. A little shoe, looked like a little shoe down there. When you hit that, you know, they fly all over on it. That's the way. Seeded broadcast. Seven and eight feet wide. It was quite a while before I ever saw a grain drill. Dad didn't like it. He never liked a drill because there was too much open ground in between the drill rows where there was nothing growing, you know.

speaker: John Eikum
words: So, we had the grain drill, or that old seeder till,

speaker: John Eikum
words: oh, it must have been nineteen. Nineteen eighteen. Nineteen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, talking about those 1893 hard times, did you tell me something about them trying to foreclose on the farms from Moscow?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Oh, yes. Yeah, that was Judge Piper.

speaker: John Eikum
words: He issued an order, and if that order went into effect, it would...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you hear your phone ringing there? Is your wife here?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Should I answer it? What did he do?

speaker: John Eikum
words: He gave an order, and if that order stood, it would mean that they'd have a...

speaker: John Eikum
words: A receiver for every ranch or every farm around there. So the farmer all got together, went up to Moscow. Well, they had a big crowd. They had a big crowd. It's clear from Main Street up to the courthouse, and that's quite a ways, you know. And they were going to hang the judge. They had the rope there, and they were going to hang the judge unless he rescinded the order. But they couldn't find the judge. He sent one of his clerks out to meet the delegation. But he rescinded the order right away because they would have hung him if they'd have found him. Because they were determined to do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Things were that bad that year, huh?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Things were that bad. That was in 1894.

speaker: John Eikum
words: So they didn't recover right away after the bad harvest. Oh well, gradually. The ones that could hang on. Now there's several that hang on. Now the place where we stayed at Hovey's, they hung on. He had quite a bit of land. He had about 240 acres. One of the bigger farms.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that Palma Hovey's father -in -law?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's the place that your father was working when you came over?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, that's where he was working. So they had a pretty good -sized farm already.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah. Well, you know, in those days you could take up a three -quarter section. You could take it up on Homestead, or you could take it up on Free Amstion, and you could take another quarter on Timber Claim. You had to plant so many trees. If you planted so many trees, you got the 160.

speaker: John Eikum
words: So the first ranchers in there, they... I know Mr. Smith. I think he used all three of his.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder what Mr. Smith thought when he lost all that land for so little money.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean the one that lost the 400 acres?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah, that one. Oh, that was Johnson. Johnson. That's who I mean.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what could he do?

speaker: John Eikum
words: He'd spent all his money beforehand. He even had a Negro cook, you know. He was one of the higher -ups. No, he had a Negro cook, and he had all kinds of servants.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Maybe he overspent then. Yeah, he overspent. That's it. Like some people do now, you know. They overspend.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But, you know, now it seemed to me that later years, they used to carry a farmer over the next year. They'd carry him for a whole year.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah, but there was no credit in those days. Like the guy said, if you went down there and bought an item for 50 cents and asked for a credit, you couldn't get it. I know Mother went out one day. She had some eggs she sent in, but she wanted a pound of coffee. That was 20 cents. And Dad had loaded up his load of wheat, and she didn't know how she was going to get that pound of coffee. As she walked down just half a mile, her Dad had loaded up. As she walked along, she walked. She happened to look down. She found a dime. In the dust. As she walked a little further, she found another dime. There she had her 20 cents to buy a coffee.

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, they had to trade their butter and eggs for their groceries.

speaker: John Eikum
words: But by 1901, everything was hunky -dory again. We lived in a little shack. It was one room and two little bedrooms off to the side, and there was 13 of us in there at one time. Ten children, and two older folks, and then my uncle stayed there too. Of course, we had a dam for them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do we call a dam?

speaker: John Eikum
words: We call it a sofa that we could open up, you know. That's where my uncle and I slept in the living room, or the living room kitchen, and general utility room all in one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the other kids? Where did the rest of the family sleep?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, they slept like hogs.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Some at the head, some at the foot. You know, on a hog sleep. Feet and feet together.

speaker: John Eikum
words: In 1909, Dad built the house. After all, the family was ready to leave. Some of them had already left. He put up a full two -story, full 18 foot to the beam with five bedrooms, four upstairs and one down, and a parlor, and a big dining room, 16 by 18, and a kitchen and a pantry. No bathroom in those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sounds like a real luxury compared to what you had.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, you really got lost in there. That's where I learned papering. I put the wall cloth and papered all the rooms. It just didn't be in good stead because after I had my operation in Portland in 1944, I couldn't do any more farming. So I went to about 150 houses in the valley of Troy and Moscow and at Lewiston. Boy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, talking about these days when all the family was living in the small space there, that's so different than for me like when I was growing up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I wonder, what was that like for everybody to be in so many kids growing up in one small space? How did that work?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, they didn't all grow up.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Of course, they had to have big families in those days. They all had big families. Ten was about the smallest number. Then it's from there up to 22. Twenty -two children. No, Cameron. Cameron, well, he lived east of Tennessee. She had 22. And no twins in the family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How could so many kids grow up? How could you care for so many kids at once?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well,

speaker: John Eikum
words: they didn't get much care. But they had to have so many spares because there were so many died off. Now, our neighbor, they lost six children with the group.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they get tuberculosis too?

speaker: John Eikum
words: My sister, older sister passed away with tuberculosis.

speaker: John Eikum
words: There.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your sister, your older sister died of tuberculosis when she was young.

speaker: John Eikum
words: She was 16 years old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what happened? What happened to her? Did it just happen all of a sudden?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, she had it for quite a while and she was just about over it. Then she went to visit one of her friends, one of her girlfriends. They had moved away. They were living down in Bedrock Canyon over here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know where that's at?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Bedrock Canyon on the other side of Leland. Oh, okay. Yeah. That big canyon that goes down there. And they were fooling around, you know, and she was just about through with it. Just about cured.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And this girl, they were out there on the lawn or out in the yard, fooling around. She turned the cold water hose on her. And she was so honored, my sister was so honored she wouldn't change clothes, you know. And she caught an awful cold and I started it up again.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I think she was the only one that passed away while we were living down there. After we got up in the big house, I had a sister of 16 years old pass away with a brain fever. They called it brain fever in those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, that's what they called it. Now they'd call it, I don't know what they'd call it now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the symptom? What happened to her?

speaker: John Eikum
words: She lost consciousness. It must be kind of a hemorrhage. Because she was going to school in town, to high school. I wasn't there at the time. It was the time I was up on a reservation on the hostess.

speaker: John Eikum
words: But I got a telegram one morning that she had passed away. That's the first I knew she was ailing. And she got it suddenly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you the oldest in the family of the kids?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, my sister that passed away, tuberculosis, was the oldest.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you the oldest boy?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yup.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, there was only two boys and eight girls in the family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have a lot of responsibilities fall on your shoulders being the oldest boy and just two boys?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, I started following the foot burner when I was 14 years old.

speaker: John Eikum
words: So, I done practically all the farm work after I was 14. But then after I was 14, when I was 21, I took up a homestead and moved away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father supervise you when you were working on the farm?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he tell you what to do? No?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Oh, he'd help. He always liked to do the seeding or the drilling himself. But I had to do all the plowing and preparing the ground.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have a lot of responsibilities?

speaker: John Eikum
words: So, I done lots of walking in my time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: With all those girls in the family, you must have eaten pretty good.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Oh, pretty fair.

speaker: John Eikum
words: There wasn't any of that prepared stuff to buy at the store in those days. Everything was in bulk, you know.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Coffee come in the great big sacks, about 200 pounds. They'd put it on the floor in the store, or it'd be open when you wanted anything. They'd weigh out a pound or two or whatever you wanted and put it in a big coffee mill they had there and grind it for you.

speaker: John Eikum
words: But you didn't get anything extra. I know the storekeeper up there. He's kind of a crippled fellow. He'd weigh out the coffee and if it weighed a little bit over, he'd pick out one coffee bean to make it balance. Same with the sugar lumps. You see the Norwegians, they're great on that sugar lump.

speaker: John Eikum
words: If it weighed too heavy, he'd take a lump out and it wouldn't be quite enough, so he'd just take a knife and he'd cut the lump in two. That made it balance. It had to be everything exact.

speaker: John Eikum
words: He'd walk for the cane. There were three brothers there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which store was this?

speaker: John Eikum
words: At that time it was Followed Brothers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: By the way, were your parents very strict with the kids, or did they let them do pretty much what they wanted to?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, you had to tell them the line.

speaker: John Eikum
words: But I was the same way. After I got married and I grew up, when I come to the house, I heard the kids say, Well, here comes Dad. They run and hide. Well, you had to be strict. Of course, we had ceiling lights that looked like a big bowl, and they used that for a basketball basket.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your mother easier going than your father?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She was easier going.

speaker: John Eikum
words: My dad was pretty strict.

speaker: John Eikum
words: He only hit me once.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I was supposed to feed the chickens early in the morning. I didn't go out there as fast as he thought I should, so he'd give them a cuff the long side of the ear.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And I was strict with the kids, just about as strict as he was, but nothing gets paid off. They're all doing pretty well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your parents treat the girls differently than the boys?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No. No, not the same.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about speaking English? Did you speak English before you started school?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No. It was pretty hard, you know, because when you was at home, you used Norwegian, and when you went to school, you used English.

speaker: John Eikum
words: But then, that was just two months, two months in the fall and two months in the spring. That's all the schooling you had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you learn much when you went to school?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No. They don't get into schooling at all. It's all athletics.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you learn much when you went to school?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah.

speaker: John Eikum
words: All that I ever knew.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I could outfigure anybody in school.

speaker: John Eikum
words: They had a long row of figures, about that long as a test. And the teacher said the first one that got his answer up to the desk and had it right would get a nickel.

speaker: John Eikum
words: So I took it home and I figured it out. But then he played a trick on them, you know. Next morning he said to take out a certain figure out of there and then figure it out. So I just subtracted the figure that he told me to take out. And I wound up with the number one.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Right now, if I could see, I could not figure any college graduate. But I learned that by playing pinocchio. Just as fast as I could shuffle the cards out, I could count them. And you count every point, like jacks and queens. The jacks are two and the queens are three and the kings are four.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you playing pinocchio when you were a kid?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Oh, yes. Well, it wasn't pinocchio, mostly. It was the old Norwegian game of twist. Old Norwegian twist.

speaker: John Eikum
words: But I was too lucky at cards.

speaker: John Eikum
words: We had one of Mother's brothers came out from Canada and her cousin came from Minnesota. They visited there one winter and we played twist.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And I beat the old, my dad and his partner.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And Mr. Braham and I, that was Ma's cousin, were partners. And we beat every game in twist.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you betting?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, no betting. That's when Dad made the remark about me. I was just a kid. I guess I was about 17 or 18. Dad got disgusted and said, the devil helps his own.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you and your dad get along a little rough at times?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah. After I grew up.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I know one time, after we got the derrick, after we built the new barn, got a cable and derrick in there and he was supposed to drive the derrick horse. Well, he was just a young colt and a three -year -old. And he'd never been driven single and Dad lost his temper. And he jerked on the line so hard that he broke the line.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I'd have lost my temper.

speaker: John Eikum
words: He says, now he says, you go down there, he says, you run your pitchfork right through that so and so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The colt?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah. So I just told him, I says, Dad, I says, you better look out or you might get a taste of the pitchfork yourself.

speaker: John Eikum
words: He sure was awful quick tempered.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, he could have led the horse to start with, you know, knew what to do after he got used to it while it was finally done.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that it was hard for him having so many kids and a big family and trying to make a living from such a rough start?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Oh yeah. He wasn't used to that, you know. Now in Norway, he never done any farm work. He was always in the government, local government, same as the county commissioner up here. He never was home. He had all hired help. You see, the land he had back there was a little bigger than the whole township. But there wasn't much farmland on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder why he decided to leave that for a little homestead land in America.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, it was too heavy a responsibility because there, you see, the oldest son always took over. And then he had to take care of his parents, his grandparents, and his great grandparents.

speaker: John Eikum
words: That's quite a load.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And there wasn't much land he could farm. It was mostly grazing land.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And then it was, that was quite a chore. Because you couldn't get off the farmstead back there unless you went in a boat. You're going to transport your cattle up to summer grazing up on top. You have to take them all in a boat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was this in Norway?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Sognefjord.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You've heard of the Sognefjord, haven't you?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'm not sure if I've heard of that one or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the nearest town?

speaker: John Eikum
words: The nearest big town is Bergen. And this was about 32 Norwegian miles northwest of Bergen. But you know, 32 Norwegian miles, a mile in Norway was equivalent to seven miles in this country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Ed Ramsdale told me he came from an area not too far from there. Because Bergen was the nearest town for his parents too.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, they had little groceries and stuff like that in front of every community, you know. Now mother was raised further in, further in the fjord. The fjord goes way into Norway. Bergen cuts it in two.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And her dad was a merchant. We called him a merchant. He had a store where they sold everything. He even sold whiskey.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it was quite a change for them to be farming in Genesee. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the land that your father had, how did it compare to most of the places around there as far as how good it was?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Oh, it was just as good. Yeah.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Of course the land in the valley itself, that was mostly bottom land, you know. Well, you know how it is up there when you go down through the valley. We lived across the hill further east. You know where the church is up there. Yeah, we're talking about on Cow Creek. Yeah. Our farm was about three quarters of a mile north and east of the church. They had to go over that first ridge.

speaker: John Eikum
words: That's where all my wife's folks lived on that other little valley that goes down. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many families were there in the Norwegian community when you were a youngster?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Oh, there must have been about 40 or 50 families living in there. You know, they only had 80 acres apiece, with the exception of that 400 -acre farm. Some had 40 acres and some had 80 acres.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, we talked about the church a little last time and I did some little reading up on it to see what I could find out. I got the 90th anniversary program for the church. It had an account of the history of its early years, its development.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, the early years was before my time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Let's see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In 1878, I think, wasn't it?

speaker: John Eikum
words: 1878. That's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was when they first started, right?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah, 1878. That was our saviors. Yeah, our saviors. That's one north of God. That's the upper church, we called it. And the lower church was the United.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: North of God is what they called the upper one?

speaker: John Eikum
words: That's what I called it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because you belonged to the lower one, right?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, I belonged to the upper one. That is funny.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Most of those that belonged to the lower church lived on the other side of the upper church, so they had to pass, you see. That was quite a... whenever they passed one church, they had to look the other way, so they didn't even want to look at the church.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And the ones up above, they had to go to the lower church and the lower ones had to go to the upper church, so they had to pass the church that they didn't like every time they went to church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they not supposed to associate with the members of the other church? Did that matter?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, it didn't matter. Of course, there was no law against it, but then it was frowned upon anyway. But we didn't take... because now, both my uncles that lived there on the flat belonged to the lower church and we belonged to the upper church. So, during Christmas, we all got together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did your family belong to the upper church instead of the lower one?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, because that's the closest.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And we thought it was awful if we ever went down to the other church. That's the worst thing.

speaker: John Eikum
words: But you know, when you compare the salaries that the minister got at that time and the salaries they get now, there's no comparison.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I know Reverend Breivik, his salary was $150 a year.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And he had the crop of 40 acres. 40 acres belonged to the parsonage. So, one day every spring, all the parishioners would go over there and put the crop in. Or they'd go over there with four or five plows and a couple of harels and a drill or two and do it all in one day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Then that crop was his?

speaker: John Eikum
words: That crop was his. But they didn't raise much. But he had a $150 salary a year.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And then the two holiday offerings belonged to the minister. Christmas and Easter took up a special offering on those days. But then he had quite a family. He had four or was it five children.

speaker: John Eikum
words: So, I guess he didn't live too high. I guess not. On that salary.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, you were telling me a little bit before about what the dispute was about between the churches. And I think this is very interesting because I think nowadays it's hard for people to understand how you can have two churches so close together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, what was the basis of the disagreement, why there were two churches instead of just one in the area?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, it's from this statement in the Bible.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Many are called, no, many are called but few are chosen.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Now, in the United Church down there, they claim that they were the chosen ones. They couldn't agree on that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did the other church say? The Agustana? The United or the Synod?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah. Well, they claim that anybody could,

speaker: John Eikum
words: anybody if they believed, they would be saved. But the other side said that only the ones that the Holy Ghost chooses will be saved.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Of course, that was handed down from the main church body, you know. They were adults too at the same time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The ones that said only those that the Holy Ghost, was that our saviors?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No. That was the other one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I see.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, that's what the controversy was over, but the main controversy was everybody wanted to be the head man, you see. The big one in the church.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Now, there's old man Mitling. He was the prime mover in the United Church.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And the Smiths were the main mover in the upper church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it was more, it got down more to two different factions? Would that be accurate to say?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: John Eikum
words: It wasn't so much their creed, but then it was differences between the two factions. Both wanted to be leaders. Nobody wanted to give in, so they just split.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In 1878, that was long before my time, what was the name of the church? Was that?

speaker: John Eikum
words: That was Christiansen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, Lebanon, wasn't it? Wasn't it called the Lebanon Church?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, let me see. I have it written down here in the history.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, the first one is called, the first one is called Our Savior's, wait a minute.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Let's see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The first one is called Our Savior's Lutheran Church. Our Savior's Lutheran. Vorfresslers Menignet. I can't pronounce it. I don't know Norwegian. The second one was called the Lebanon Trinity Church. And the first one was called Our Savior's.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Oh, the first church that was built. That's the one that was turned into a barn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever see that barn?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No. Yeah, that was built first. That's when they had the split, you know. But you belong to our saviors. Our saviors.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And our saviors said that anyone who believed would be saved. I read in here that there was a dispute between the synods, that our saviors was aligned to the old country Norwegian church and also to the Missouri synod. And that the other became part of the anti -Missouri synod. So that figured into it because I guess it says in here that when they agreed to combine into a single synod that the same year, 1918 or 1917. 1917.

speaker: John Eikum
words: In order to make a go out they have to combine.

speaker: John Eikum
words: It said in here that also that the young people were really the leaders as far as breaking down the barriers between the two churches.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember that?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Oh yes, they had their young people's society. That was mostly in our saviors' luthier. But then the young people from the United Church, they called it the United Trinity. They'd come to our young people's society, you know, because we had a big society. I guess they had about 70 or 80. Till the kids get together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do when you got together?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Oh, they had their program and then they played games.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Had refreshments.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I was president for one year of the young people's society up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would the program be, for example?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Old recitations and songs.

speaker: John Eikum
words: It was mostly like another program, like to have a school program.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the group mostly serious or was it mostly fun for the kids?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mostly fun.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, there were several of them.

speaker: John Eikum
words: There was one family that wouldn't have anything to do with them, you know. They wouldn't come out to anything. And you know when they went together, the records of our saviors were lost somewhere.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Because a member of the lower group, or what you'd call the United Trinity, got ahold of them. He wanted to see the records of our saviors. And our secretary turned the records over to them and they disappeared. They couldn't find them again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it was a mistake or just it could have been on purpose?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, I wouldn't say for sure, but they were strictly for the Trinity church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what happened when the churches combined? How that worked?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, I wasn't here at the time. I was in the service at the time. See, I went into the service in 17.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I didn't get back until 19. When you came back, there was just one congregation. One congregation. It went alright. Same as they are now, I guess. Of course, all the old warrior heads are gone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were young, did you see where there was any difference between the two churches? Could you see any difference?

speaker: John Eikum
words: I couldn't see any difference, but it felt different.

speaker: John Eikum
words: You could see the difference, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how the difference felt to you?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, you looked down upon them, upon the ones in the Trinity church, and I guess they felt the same about what it was in our Savior's church.

speaker: John Eikum
words: But it finally had to die out gradually.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say you called it North of God, why did you call it, why did it have a name like that?

speaker: John Eikum
words: It's just myself that calls it that, you know. Same as the Methodist church. The Methodist church North and the Methodist church South. So one is North of God and the other is South of God. I see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I see. But that's somewhere in between them.

speaker: John Eikum
words: There is such a thing as the Methodist South and the Methodist North.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes, on American Ridge in Latah County they had them both on different ends of the ridge. Only the Methodist North was on the south end of the ridge and the Methodist South was on the north end of the ridge. And I know one lady when she was a girl couldn't figure out why the Methodist North was on the south and the Methodist South was on the north.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, when you have it that way, when you have a Methodist North and a Methodist South, somebody has to be between the two of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These two families that were, each one of them was sort of big in the other church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what those families were like? Were they leaders in the community?

speaker: John Eikum
words: They wanted to be, yes.

speaker: John Eikum
words: This one at Trinity, he was, he wanted to be named King.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And in the church they were mostly all related. See the Tegelunds and the Smiths and the Bargains were all related.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Intermarried.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder if it was the same at the South Church. They were intermarried too.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Not so much. Not so much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they ever talk about the old ministers, Thormesgaard and Christiansen? What they had been like?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No.

speaker: John Eikum
words: It didn't mention in our Savior's records, it mentioned.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Let's see, Thomasgaard. Oh, Thomasgaard, yes.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I was trying to think of the other one up in the Doherty Church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, Christiansen was the first.

speaker: John Eikum
words: He was there until... Thomasgaard came. ... 1984. Yeah. But when he came in 1993, in our Savior's, it was Tennyson. Yeah. Tennyson and then I think it was Martin Christiansen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was the other Christiansen's son.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And by the time I was confirmed, it took three of them to get me confirmed.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I started with Reverend Anderson, Christian Anderson. And when he left, he was called to another church back east.

speaker: John Eikum
words: So we had Reverend Mohen come and rehearse. Reverend Mohen from Spokane and Reverend Bleckardt from Rockford. They came down once a week. And by the time I was to be confirmed, they had called a new minister, Reverend O .C. Heligston. So it took four of them to confirm me. You were a hard case. Must have been.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How important was the church and the community to the people living there in the early days?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, I don't think it was as important then as it is now.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Of course, they'd go out to church, go to communion in the morning, and work their fields in the afternoon.

speaker: John Eikum
words: One farmer had his hired men stay at home, do the work, and he'd go to church and go to communion.

speaker: John Eikum
words: As far as that goes, I don't think there's, oh, there might be a few of them that take it seriously now, but...

speaker: John Eikum
words: If they're standing in the community, they figure out, they're perceived in the community. That's why they attend church regularly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Nowadays, you mean?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You don't think it was that way in the early days?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, not so much.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Because they hadn't quite got away from what they had in Norway. Most of those old divers came from Norway in the first place.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And anybody that belonged to the church, like the minister or the bishop especially, if you happened to be out walking out on the road and met the bishop or any of the county or precinct,

speaker: John Eikum
words: you had to step aside, step off the road, take off your hat, and bow, and he went by.

speaker: John Eikum
words: That's like the fellow sitting back there. He wouldn't take his hat off to the sheriff. He said even if he had nothing but a ragged old cap on his head.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Somebody said that in Genesee?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, that's a little country in Norway. Here comes your wife.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go to church in those days and sit through the services?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sure.

speaker: John Eikum
words: They all just stayed through the services. Now the kids go first, you know, and then they go home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did everybody wear their best clothes to church?

speaker: John Eikum
words: They have to dress up to go to church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say that it was quite a social occasion then too for the community? Or was it strictly religious and that's it?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Oh, they had social.

speaker: John Eikum
words: If you'd call it social.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I know at our place all the bastards there, four or five bastards come over every Sunday and they'd all bring a full quart of alcohol along.

speaker: John Eikum
words: They'd socialize for a while, but after a while they got so they wasn't able to do anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the most important thing you had to remember?

speaker: John Eikum
words: We had a nice place for them to meet.

speaker: John Eikum
words: We had a square, I wouldn't call it a building, but it was poles set up and down, you know. And it was planted hops all around. In the summer, that hops would all go up over there and you'd have regular armor in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that by the church?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, this was at our place, home place. Oh. No, they had no... all they had at the church on Sundays was my services.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Services at eleven o 'clock in the morning. And when the young people met, they'd meet out at the different houses for their social gathering. That would last all the way from eight o 'clock to midnight.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would people do there? Would they be dancing?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, no dancing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No dancing?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was frowned upon.

speaker: John Eikum
words: That was forbidden to dance.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What could you do? Did you play games?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They played games.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Parlor games. Parlor games, played them outside.

speaker: John Eikum
words: The transportation was good as well. They had to stage lines for Spokane to Mount Idaho. You run down through the valley about once a week or once every two weeks. Of course, it took quite a while for a small cat to Mount Idaho with horses.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I guess you heard of Felix Warren. Yes, I have. Asshole stage driver.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he like? Do you remember him?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, I was too young. I remember him. I'll show you a picture of him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what I wanted to ask you about?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wanted you to tell me that story about the two suicides on the same night. That seemed so incredible to me that such a thing could happen on the same night.

speaker: John Eikum
words: We were quite shocked. See, my sister was taking instructions to go to confirmation and she went out the morning of February the 14th. That's St. Valentine's Day.

speaker: John Eikum
words: After she came home, she was all out of rest to tell us the news, you know.

speaker: John Eikum
words: One farmer had hung himself down at the barn and this other farmer had gone over into the barn belonging to this fella that hung himself at the other fella's barn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Tell me, what was it that happened to each of those men and why did they kill themselves? Why did they?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, this first one, he came out from North Dakota. He'd been a sheriff back there. He'd hung quite a few men back there in the Dakotas when he was sheriff. He was quite a gambler. He'd been in town and they'd gambled and he'd lost all his money and he also lost a family cow to one of his neighbors.

speaker: John Eikum
words: So when he went home that night, it was a little snow on the ground and he'd been walking back and forth between his house and this other place where he hung himself. It was a solid trail, a track through the snow where he'd been walking back and forth, studying about this all night, I guess.

speaker: John Eikum
words: So when this farmer come to, about to his barn, he opened the door and there was the old man standing. He was dead, but of course he didn't realize it because this fella had long whiskers, you know, and hid in the rope. And his toes just about reached the floor.

speaker: John Eikum
words: So he told them the night before I took them home from town, they'd come down next morning and they'd go in and straighten things out. He grabbed a hold of his hand to say good morning and shake hands with them and his hand was stiff. It gave him quite a shock. He didn't live on that place long after that. He sold out. His father and his father's wife discussed with his wife. They didn't know what they should do with the old man because it was getting to be such a burden to them. And the old man had overheard that. So I guess he just figured if he was in the way, he'd fix it so he'd get out of the way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he went down to this other fella's barn, the same one that killed himself and the other man?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: John Eikum
words: We often wondered what would have happened if they'd happened to me in one place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They didn't know about the other one at all?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, no. They didn't know about one at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The man that shook the dead man's hand, was he a reverend? Did you say he was a reverend?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was just going to help him out?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah, he was just going to help him out. Go down and straighten things out next morning.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would straighten things out mean? Try to get a loan for a guy or something?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, I don't know. They said he'd straighten it out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would you do if he had money?

speaker: John Eikum
words: I don't think anybody would loan him any money. Because the first thing you do if he had any money, he'd go to the gambling table again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He had a wife and children?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yes, he had a wife. I see there was Oscar and Eleanor.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I think he had four more children.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were these two men very good friends?

speaker: John Eikum
words: They didn't hardly know each other.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But they were neighbors?

speaker: John Eikum
words: They were neighbors.

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, when you get to be 80 years old, you don't get around very much.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And one belonged to the Trinity Lutheran, the other our Savior's Lutheran.

speaker: John Eikum
words: So they didn't mingle much there either, going to church. They were two different factions.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did people think about suicide in those days?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They must have thought, I imagine they would have been pretty disturbed by something like that. No, yes.

speaker: John Eikum
words: But that year there were so many of them that there was at least five suicides in Genesee that year, or in the country around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what year that was then?

speaker: John Eikum
words: They were 19 -1. Because my sister was born in 1887 and she was going on 15. So 57 or 87 to 1 would be 14.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: 15 when she was confirmed?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah, she was confirmed in the summer afterwards or late spring afterwards. So I guess it was about 19 -1, St. Valentine's Day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You told me about another suicide in there and I'd like you to tell me that again. That was in Genesee.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And that's part of the Genesee Valley too.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I don't know what his trouble was. They found him sitting in a chair. Even on a livery barn, they found him sitting dead in a chair. He shot himself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There was that other fella, we won't use his name, but you said that he killed himself over a girl.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, this was the one down at the livery barn. This other one had family troubles.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And there were no such thing as getting divorced in those days, you know. If you had divorced, you were disgraced forever.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And now, nowadays, it's a common thing. That's right.

speaker: John Eikum
words: You know, there used to be two Genesees at one time, Old Town and New Town.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I told you how that happened.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did it happen?

speaker: John Eikum
words: It's when the railroad built in there. They asked too much for the land where Old Town was already started. So they just moved back a mile and started Genesee as it is now.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Then they had to move all their buildings and stuff from Old Town over to the new site.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was it that asked for too much money?

speaker: John Eikum
words: The farmers around there.

speaker: John Eikum
words: See, there was Mr. Walby, he was one of the farmers there. And Mr. Jim Hansen was another one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So if they had moved the depot over there a mile farther east, did you find her?

speaker: John Eikum
words: She had just called Skett, so she had to ride home with Skett. So I did that for nothing. Bad luck today. Today is a bad luck day. You could have called up, but I guess you wouldn't have gotten a hold of them anyhow down there at the Safeways. Probably not, because they are on the paint valve.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they pretty much had to rebuild the town then?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, they moved some buildings over. Moved them by oxen, yokel oxen. I know Paul Mahanson's father helped them move some buildings over. And there was no bridge. Across the creek there, the house got stuck in the creek overnight, so they just left it there. Went and got drunk during the night, so I guess they moved the next morning.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that a store or a house they were moving?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was a store.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I think it was an old Jake Rosensteed store.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Speaking of Jake Rosensteed, what was he like, the way you remember him?

speaker: John Eikum
words: He was a little short with a white mustache.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Wore a pair of glasses. I was in the store several times.

speaker: John Eikum
words: He had a son and two daughters.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Max, Max Rosensteed and...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mom, do you remember the Rosensteed girls' names?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, I didn't know them. Well, I knew both of them.

speaker: John Eikum
words: But they moved to New York. The girls did. An old man moved on to California, I guess. He came back to Genesee one time and he was talking about Genesee. He says, All this town needs, he says, is a post office and a service station.

speaker: John Eikum
words: You know, when he left, there was eight or nine stores in Genesee. All kinds of businesses, but now there's nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a very good businessman?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He seems to have a reputation for being pretty good.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, he was a Jew. There ought to be a good businessman.

speaker: John Eikum
words: See, that town was settled by Jews. Old Simon Levi, he run a warehouse.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I talked with a relation person. If he was alive, he could tell you lots of stories. He lived to be 102. He came there in 18... 1878 to 79.

speaker: John Eikum
words: He passed away up at the Coeur d 'Alene homes. He could tell you about old times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he know Levi?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Sure, he knew Levi. He knew Drake Rosestein and the other Jews. There was another Jew there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mentioned him before, somebody else that was there, a pioneer? The Jews?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah, you said somebody else besides Levi. There's one by the name of Alexander and London.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Alexander and London.

speaker: John Eikum
words: They had a grocery store, Alexander and London. They weren't partners. And this old fellow, he said, you know, they had to haul their wheat clear to Lewiston before they got the railroad into Genesee. Their nearest trading place was Lewiston. So he said the Jews would stand down here on the sidewalk and they had glasses. They'd see the farmers coming up there, they'd get together and decide what they was going to pay for the wheat.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Got about 24 cents a bushel, I guess. 25 cents a bushel. And it took them two days to deliver a load.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Two days to get here? Or one day here and one back?

speaker: John Eikum
words: One day down and one day back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, you're talking about...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: ...for being fair?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And honest in Genesee?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah. He'd never tell you the price of anything. You'll go in there and look at an article and ask him how much he wanted for it. Oh, he says it's reasonable. It's reasonable. That's all the answer you got out of him. It was reasonable. It was reasonable.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't he have the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: ...about the first safe in Genesee?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I heard he had the safe that kept people's valuables sort of... ...almost like a little bank. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've heard his son Mox was sort of a hell raiser.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, Mox was, yeah. He'd go to Spokane, spend a week or so.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well... The ranchers knew when Mox went to Spokane... ...they'd come in and want to pay their bills. And they made old... ...Jake mad. He sure cussed that kid for running off. But then, you know, if Mox was there, they'd never show up to pay their bills. But they knew he was gone. And he was a bookkeeper. An old man couldn't do anything without Mox.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Pretty smart.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Pretty smart.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Did he loan... I loaned one time... ...a big Dutchman. However, he went to Moscow... ...and bought a heck load of groceries. So then he'd come into Rosestein next day... ...and wanted to buy a sack of sugar.

speaker: John Eikum
words: So he told Jake, he says... I went to Moscow yesterday. I should have got the sugar up there, he said... ...but I had so much groceries in my heck I didn't have room for the sugar. So Jake says, yeah, that's the way. He said, that's the way. You go to Moscow, he says, spend your cash up there, then you come down here... ...and ask me for a sack of sugar and charge it.

speaker: John Eikum
words: He was after the money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did most of the Norwegians trade? Whose store did they use?

speaker: John Eikum
words: At the beehive. George Hobson.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hobson?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Hobson, that's O -B -S -O -N. George Hobson. Hobson.

speaker: John Eikum
words: That was a cheap store, you know. You could get calico for three cents a yard, five cents. But then the big merchants were London and... Alexander and London.

speaker: John Eikum
words: But they sold out the Pollard brothers. That's where we did all our trading, was the Pollard brothers. That's old Alexander and London store.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did most of the Norwegians trade with the beehive or with the Follocks?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: With Follocks.

speaker: John Eikum
words: In later years. I told you about that guy that would pick out a coffee bean or a little sugar. Yeah. That's the place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did they trade there instead of Rosenstein's, say? Was the prices better or what?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, no, they had a better stock. See, Rosenstein had a little low -down store stuck back in the hillside and it was pretty dark in there. You couldn't see a darn thing in there. And these were brick buildings on Main Street. Of course, he was on Main Street. He was on the west end of Main.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Right across from the Livery Barn. That's the Livery Barn on Main Street.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I guess they moved the Livery Barn and put up a brick there. They now have a parkland house there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you spend much time in Genesee when you were a young man?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Oh, well, I spent most of my time out on the rags. On the farm.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I didn't go to town, but Dad went to work on the building, this railroad up the river. He was gone for two or three weeks. I had to walk to town.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I made quite a few trips walking to town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How about coffee? Have you had any lunch?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah, I've had lunch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: John, are you getting dry in the throat from talking? Want a cup of tea?

speaker: John Eikum
words: I don't talk very much. Not really. I'll have my coffee.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, I'll get you one.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Okay, two cups of tea. She doesn't have her coffee. That's all they do is coffee and yak.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that much different than it was in the early days with women?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, they get together once in a while. Of course, it wasn't quite as easy to get around as it is now.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Just the nearest neighbors come and visit once in a while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was going to town much of an occasion in those days for people in the country? What was special?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Saturday, Saturday afternoons mostly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Trading? Would they trade then?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah, they'd trade. That's when they'd done their trading.

speaker: John Eikum
words: After a while,

speaker: John Eikum
words: we got so we'd go to Moscow.

speaker: John Eikum
words: You could drive to Moscow in an hour and a half if you had a fast team.

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, they didn't drink coffee like they drink coffee now, you know. They'd make a pot in the morning and they'd drink on that all day long. And they got their final fine squirts that's in the bottom of the candy just as black, black as tar. And they never throw the grounds out.

speaker: John Eikum
words: They'd just put new coffee on the old grounds. Till the can got somebody's grounds in it, it wouldn't hold any more water.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what do you remember most about the town of Genesee?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Tell me a little bit more about what the town was like in those early days when you were just a youngster.

speaker: John Eikum
words: When I was just a youngster, well, I didn't go to town very much, but I know they had, let's see, one, two, three, four, five, six saloons. And they were all pretty well patronized.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And they had seven, they had six grocery stores. And a couple of dry goods stores and two hardware stores, or three, three hardware stores.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And they had a jeweler and a millinery shop and real estate, insurance.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And they even had a lawyer. It sounds like quite a town. It was a live town then.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Especially at election time.

speaker: John Eikum
words: These candidates would come down, you see, and they knew they would go and be in town. And all the ranchers would come in there because then they'd follow the, it was all free drinks. From one saloon to the other. And they'd have this whole herd of farmers following him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The candidate would stand the drinks? The candidate would stand drinks all around?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Sure, that's the way he advertised.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the, what?

speaker: John Eikum
words: The drinks were cheap at that time. You know you could go down to the brewery, they had a brewery in Genesee too, the Genesee Brewery. You could get a glass of beer for a nickel, a big schooner for a nickel and a sandwich thrown in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the politics of the town very strong one way or the other, Democrat or Republican?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh yes, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a Republican town mostly or what?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Supposed to be, well, you see, Layton County is supposed to be a Republican county.

speaker: John Eikum
words: They had mostly Republicans. So Genesee was a Republican town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about, how much did people of one nationality, like the Norwegians or Germans, how much did they mix with the ordinary townspeople?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, they mixed alright, the same as they do now. There was no difference. Only during the First World War, there was quite a difference. If you was of German, German descent, you had to be pretty careful what you said or they'd pick you up.

speaker: John Eikum
words: The old road boss we had was a German. Every time he'd talk, he'd look around, see if anybody was listening to him. You know, he says, I have to be pretty careful what I say.

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, there was no difference. They mingled.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've heard about there being some pretty strong feeling against Germans during that war.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah, there was. You had to be pretty careful what they said.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I don't know if their phones were bugged or anything, but out in the open, they had to say.

speaker: John Eikum
words: We had some arguments. He was a road boss, this German.

speaker: John Eikum
words: So, the news come out in the paper that the Germans took the dead soldiers and rendered their bodies to get to Greece, you know, to make ammunition.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He says, you think the Germans ain't civilized yet?

speaker: John Eikum
words: He says.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It'd be tough if you were from a country that was at war with the United States, I would think.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, you see, he came over. He emigrated from Germany years ago and lived in Minnesota. Then he moved out west. He bought that place, those 400 acres, he bought that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: From the bank?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Incidentally, you mentioned Balmer getting a lot of that land.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: During 1993, I guess Balmer, people didn't like him very well, did they?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, they didn't like him. You see, they borrowed, he had the bank, Bank in Genesee. He run the bank there. And I guess they borrowed a few dollars from him and they couldn't pay him back that hard a year, so he just took over. Dozens and dozens of buyers around there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You take sugar?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, thanks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just plain black tea, no?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, actually, I could take any sugar in my tea. I think always coffee. I don't take sugar in my tea. I'd take a little in my tea.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I thought you went heavy on the sugar. No. But you're up among the Swedes up there. They use lots of sugar. Well, they do, but I don't. They use lots of sugar in their coffee.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But, um...

speaker: John Eikum
words: I know when, years ago, when they had to board in the house, the thrashing crews, she hated the Swedes when they come around. She says, you clean out their cups and there's half a cup of sugar after they drank their coffee out of it, so it didn't all dissolve.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You've been speaking in a microphone today, huh?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yep. You keep going over there. You didn't get half as much as you did last time, though. It's kind of hot there. Maybe you better let it sit a minute. That's an awful small tray. Don't miss it now. I wouldn't want to name any names. Well, it's just as well often to keep names out of it. The important thing is the stories. Yeah, that's right. Well, it was pretty late when I came to Tennessee, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, but compared to most people that are still alive, it was pretty early.

speaker: John Eikum
words: It was 82 years ago since I came to Tennessee, and that isn't very long.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well... But, some men came here in 18...

speaker: John Eikum
words: ...1878, 1877. You know, they had quite an exciting time. 1877, Indian uprising. That's when they built the fort out there east of Tennessee. I remember the fort.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they really...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: they were really afraid of being attacked in that war, huh?

speaker: John Eikum
words: That was long before my time, but the fort was still there after we came to the country. It was a big heavy log set up on end, right close together, you know. Just solid.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it being used for anything? Did kids play in it or anything?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, it was out there in the middle of the field. About a mile and a half east of the town of Genesee.

speaker: John Eikum
words: You talked about... No, that was in case the Indians come, then they'd go to the fort for safety.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Outside of that, it was abandoned.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You talk about the thrashing crews.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They had some pretty good size ones, didn't they, around Genesee?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah, pretty good size. The crews... See, the big outfits had eight bundle wagons.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And they took four pictures.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And they had to separate them in. Let's see... It took about... up to 20, 24 men.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And that was quite a bunch to get into your house when they boarded in the houses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they board in the houses?

speaker: John Eikum
words: I thought they had...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Cook wagons?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Oh, that came later. Cook wagons came a whole lot later. But to start with, they boarded in the house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So each farm wife had to prepare the meals for them while they were there?

speaker: John Eikum
words: She generally got help from the neighbor girls. I know we had seen a horseman, or seen a hoblod. She always helped with the cooking.

speaker: John Eikum
words: It was a long time before they got the cook wagons.

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, I remember in six weeks, the place we stayed always... You see, the crew never went home. They stayed there for six weeks. I guess he had to butcher a couple of cows and a hog or two. And it all went. Well, you take 20, 24 men feeding for six weeks. That's the worst I ever heard. I never heard about that before.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, that's because of the rain. That's because that was when it was raining. And they had to stay there until...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: they were planning on staying until the rain stopped, right?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah. That was 1893. Mm -hmm.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, I guess they didn't have much to eat at home, and they lived good there. They all came down from Troy, you know. That's where all the harvest hands came from.

speaker: John Eikum
words: These homesteaders up around Troy. I guess they took a homestead or timber claim or whatever they did when they moved out of the Genesee country or was pushed out of the Genesee country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Quite a few of those people around Troy did come from Genesee originally, huh?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Then they'd go back and work in the harvest to make some money to get through the winter. Yeah, get a gum stake for the winter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So Genesee was just considerably more advanced than these other parts of the county that were still getting started.

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, Troy didn't like the name they had, so they changed it to Troy. It was Walmer for a long time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They didn't like him either.

speaker: John Eikum
words: They didn't like Walmer very much either. No, and same way over in the Friar they didn't like Walmer. There was a Walmer, Walmer -Ilowe over there, you know, and they changed it to Craigmont.

speaker: John Eikum
words: There was two towns, one on each side of the railroad track over at Craigmont. One was Ilowe and one was Walmer. Walmer was on that left -hand side going that way and Ilowe was on the right -hand side of the track.

speaker: John Eikum
words: They decided they'd unite and make one town out of it and call it Craigmont.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know how Walmer came into Lewiston? How?

speaker: John Eikum
words: He walked.

speaker: John Eikum
words: He walked into Lewiston with a five -gallon keg of whiskey on his shoulder and started selling booze to the Indians. That's where he made a start.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder what kind of reputation he has in Lewiston.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I think Lewiston has forgotten all about him.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Only thing they have a park name for him, Walmer Park.

speaker: John Eikum
words: But they want to change that to the city park.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to all the land that Walmer got from the foreclosures around Genesee? Did he sell it off or what?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He sold it off.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I don't know.

speaker: John Eikum
words: There's two right across from the church.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Knutson and Mottland.

speaker: John Eikum
words: That's all sold.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Later some of these fellas that come from the east, you know, they sold out back east and they had a little money and they bought the land. And then as they got a little more money on their farming operations, then they'd buy some more land, joining land.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Now you see her brothers, they started with farming 82 acres. And during the First World War, they expanded to 1400 acres. Of course they rented all of it. They rented land and then they started buying land. First they bought 72 acres and they bought 80 acres. And his brother bought 235 acres.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Then they all farmed together until they had all their debts paid and the money ahead and then they split up.

speaker: John Eikum
