words: One of them passed away suddenly a little over a year ago, two years ago, I guess it was two years ago. He was out helping his brother paint the fence around the barnyard. And he told his brother, he says, I don't feel very well. The older brother says, well you better go to the house and lie down and rest a while, you might feel better. Well he says, I'll come and look to you in a little while. He waited 20 minutes and he went to see. His brother had gone down in the basement, laid down on the couch they had down in the basement. He found him dead at the foot of the stairs.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And her younger brother, her youngest brother, he got his start in Alaska. But he didn't get much of a start out there. He worked for the Juneau mining company up there. But then he came back to Tennessee Valley and he started a dairy herd.

speaker: John Eikum
words: But he had hard luck. He had three or four big stacks of hay right close to his dairy barn, you know. And they went in for dinner, to eat dinner, and they looked out and the stacks were afire.

speaker: John Eikum
words: He lost all his hay but he didn't lose any buildings. They put cables around the hay stacks and just drug them off with a tractor, you know.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And I know how that started out. Of course they said it couldn't start that way. But the fellow that he held had him stack the hay. He always smoked his pipe while they were stacking hay. You know it was easy enough for a spark to fall down in that dry hay and it started. And that was an awful day. That's when the big hailstorm made through there. My uncle lost all his crop.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year was this?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Roughly. Oh, it must have been in 19...

speaker: John Eikum
words: They moved out there on the plate in 1943. They moved out there on the farm straight in 1943.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who? Theon?

speaker: John Eikum
words: 43. Well, it's in 1944.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year was it?

speaker: John Eikum
words: 19. Thea's storm went through there. You're just ready to get the combiners in there. They were coming in the evening and this happened in the afternoon. Left nothing but stubble about that high. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Listen, I want to ask you about how you came to Homestead yourself up on the Coeur d 'Alene reservation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the story behind that?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, the story behind that, in 1908 they opened the... they opened three reservations. They opened the Cobble Reservation and the Flathead Reservation and the Coeur d 'Alene Reservation. So you went up to... I went to Coeur d 'Alene and registered for the Coeur d 'Alene Reservation. They give you a... then they had a drawing. And I happened to get one late number. 2 ,438 was my number.

speaker: John Eikum
words: So I got a piece of land but I didn't know anything about timber but I could have had a good timber claim, you know. White pine timber. The best claim up there, where I was on the Altar Creek Country.

speaker: John Eikum
words: It had over 10 million of big white pine and 7 or 8 million of mixed timber.

speaker: John Eikum
words: He proved up, lived there about a year and then he sold out to the lumber company for $16 ,000.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You could have gotten white pine but you didn't?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why not?

speaker: John Eikum
words: I didn't know anything about timber. I could have got a claim there. I think he sold out. Of course he didn't have a number. Then after all the numbers were drawn, they waited so long and then they threw it open for anybody to go in there and take a homestead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what kind of land did you pick?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Scrub brush.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I was looking for farmland. I wasn't looking for timber land.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I could have had 160 acres there that sold for $8 ,000.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you got the land, what did you do with it? Was it hard to clear the scrub brush?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, it was mostly large pole pine. A little about 2 or 3 inches through.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you say? Say that again?

speaker: John Eikum
words: One raised hell, he said, and the other one just raised an umbrella. That's all they raised on the homesteads.

speaker: John Eikum
words: That's the way they talked. The officials talked too because that was a rough town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What town is this?

speaker: John Eikum
words: St. Mary's. Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you think it was a rough place? Why was it different from the place?

speaker: John Eikum
words: They had a population of 900 when I went up there in 1910. And it had 7 saloons. And they all were doing big business.

speaker: John Eikum
words: See the lumberjacks, they'd work 30 days. And they'd go into town and then they'd stay a week and spend it all right there in town. Until 1917. In 1917, it turned around. They refused to spend any money in St. Mary's.

speaker: John Eikum
words: They'd work a month, a month and a half or two months. Then they'd get their grub stake and they'd come into St. Mary's. Well, they'd leave about from $25 to $50 there with Oliver Neeson to take care of until they'd come back from Spokane. Because if they knew then they went to Spokane, they'd be broke before they got out of there. So he charged 10 % for taking care of their money.

speaker: John Eikum
words: He was a Jew. Oliver Neeson.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was his religion? Or he was just a Jew because he took their money?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was a Jew.

speaker: John Eikum
words: He was a Jew. You could come in there on Monday morning if you were the first customer in there. You could get any article you wanted at your own price. Because he believed that he had to make a sale. The first man that'd come in in the morning, he had to make a sale or you wouldn't have any luck all week.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that a Jewish idea? A Jewish custom?

speaker: John Eikum
words: He wouldn't have any trade all week if he didn't make a sale to the first customer that'd come in on Monday morning.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And he was a Jew. I went in there and bought a pair of rubbers. They were $1 .25. One of these light rubbers on my shoe, you know. $1 .25. Another fellow come in and he looked at it and he says, you better have a pair of rubbers that didn't put your whip out. Well, he said maybe I should have. He took them to the side and he listened to them. He says, these rubbers certainly sell for $1 .75. He said, but you're a good customer of mine. I'll let you have it for $1 .25. Well, that's just what I paid. He thought he was getting a big bargain.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you build your own cabin on the bridge?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah, I built mine. Mom, I was too much. I found the cabin I built up there.

speaker: John Eikum
words: All I could raise was strawberries. It was a great strawberry country. I had sent down to Hood River, Oregon for 100 plants of strawberries and the most got away with 25 of them. And I had 75 plants left and I picked half a crate of strawberries every other day. I even took some down to her mother's. I picked half a crate and took down one thing like drove down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far were you from Sanders? Is that the closest town?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah, that was my training. I was 10 miles north of Sanders. There's the big cabin. I'll be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Looks like an old -time cabin, doesn't it?

speaker: John Eikum
words: I'm a good picture of John and I.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's quite a few years ago, you know. Yeah, that's quite a nice picture. About 1924 or something. John looks like a regular young rape with that hat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you enjoy living up there? How did that compare with Genesee as a place to live?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Oh, it didn't make much of an effort.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Up there?

speaker: John Eikum
words: I had a good time up there. We had a dance every night, every Saturday night. We danced clear till 8 o 'clock next morning. And then we'd go home and brush up a little Sunday morning. Then we'd go to Sunday school afterwards. Of course, we didn't have any minister or anything, but then we had this international pamphlet they sent out. Sunday school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much of a community there?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yes. And then in the afternoon we'd have a ball game every day. From April, the first of April, till the last of September.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Every day?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah, we had two teams. That's all they had to do, holding down the homestead, you know. We had two teams, the East and the West.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, there was quite a community.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it? East and west of what? Of the valley?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Of the valley.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Of the Alder Creek, right?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Alder Creek Valley. See, Alder Creek ran right through the valley and out and joined the St. Mary's River about 10 or 12 miles above St. Mary's on the St. Mary's River. That's where Alder Creek come out. That ran all the year long. It's cold, cold mountain water. It's cold, it'd hit you in the forehead if you drank it real quick.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it hard to make a go on that homestead or was it fairly easy to build a place and keep going year after year?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Oh no, not too bad. Because grocers were cheap then, you know. It didn't take much of a grub stay. If you had $100, you could live like a king all year long.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you work out at all while you were there?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Oh yes. That's where I got crippled up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't you know I was crippled?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No. I crashed the left foot. I got a log right across there. You see, I tried to draw my foot out and I had it tanted like that when the log hit it. Broke all the bones here in my instep.

speaker: John Eikum
words: The way it happened, it just started a sleigh haul in the wintertime. Well, the skids were frozen. I was frost on the skids. I grabbed the log and held it, but it shoved me right backwards to start the deck. And there was just one place, one saddle left.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what the saddle is?

speaker: John Eikum
words: A distance between two logs like that. Why you put two logs together, why there's a kind of a little trough there. There's just that one trough left. If it hadn't been for that, I'd have been better off because the log had rolled right over me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you working for somebody else?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah, I was working for a gypo.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what happened to you then?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, they took me sled as far as Benno. Then he got his old four -hour Dodge car. They got the Dodge car and he's going to take me down to the hospital in St. Mary's. And he got down as far as the cemetery by St. Mary's and he run out of gas.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And he didn't know what to do, so he saw a couple up there. Now it was in the middle of the winter and in the middle of the night, and there was a couple up there making love out in the cemetery.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And it was putting me zero. So he got that guy to take him down to St. Mary's and get some gas, put his car and made the way in. This happened about 2 .30 in the afternoon and I got into the hospital about 11 .30 that night.

speaker: John Eikum
words: So they didn't do anything that night. She'd just come with a hypo and that didn't affect me at all. She'd come back in about 20 minutes and it hadn't taken effect. She said, I don't know what to do with you. I guess I'll have to get a hammer and knock you in the head to knock you out. And that's in the hospital from the ninth day of January until the fifth day of May.

speaker: John Eikum
words: But that's the best time of my life. All those nice nurses taking care of you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about your foot?

speaker: John Eikum
words: We'd go down after the doctor so I could walk on crutches, use crutches. There was four of us, all of our crutches, we'd go down and play pool.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And in between time I'd send downtown and get some cream puffs and a quart of ice cream and then the nurses and I would go in the linen room and have an ice cream social.

speaker: John Eikum
words: See there was no special regulations up there. It's a lumberjack hospital. It belonged to the Western Hospital Association. Didn't cost me anything. I was getting paid 50 % of my wages besides. My wages weren't very big. They were four dollars a day. So I got two dollars a day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to the homestead?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you give it up after you proved up on it?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, I sold it. I sold it for, what was it, 250 or 300 dollars. Sold it to one of the neighbors. But he was a financier. He went downtown. He got, he bought 160, he bought another 160. He went downtown to the bank and borrowed 2 ,500 on the whole works. And then he skipped out to Spokane and let the banker hold the sack.

speaker: John Eikum
words: That's the only way you make any money in this world. I found that out but it's too late.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well maybe it's better not to make any money if that's the only way you can do it.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I wanted to ask you about World War I a little bit too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About your experience overseas.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You told me that you were in this fortress, an underground fortress?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah, that was all that we're doing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like to be in this fortress?

speaker: John Eikum
words: It wasn't a fortress. It was just a big underground cave. It was all cemented up on the sides and had alleys going out on either side. But there was no light in there. No light at all. The lights came on at noon and an hour in the evening. Outside of that you was in total darkness.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Of course the cooks prepared the soup out at the entrance to the underground cave. And the big shells would come over you know, stir up the sand so you got half soup and half sand.

speaker: John Eikum
words: You told me there was a lot of rats in there too. Oh yes, every time you lay down in the middle of the day the rats would scamper all over you.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well I'll tell you, in nine months I was overseas. But I was overseas twelve months, or eleven months. In nine months, by the time I took one bath.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And in eight months I never sat down at a table to eat. You'd stand around outside.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about changing your clothes? Did men ever get to change their uniforms at all?

speaker: John Eikum
words: You never took your clothes off. You spread your thin old deep blankets on the ground and lay down on it and put the other blanket over you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It sounds like pretty tough conditions. Worse than lumber camps.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Worse than the lumber camps used to be. Now the lumberjacks are dressed like a hotel. You have clean sheets and pillowcases every week.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were you doing in this underground...

speaker: John Eikum
words: Just laid there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But what was the point of having all the men be there anyway?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well we were in reserve.

speaker: John Eikum
words: All we'd done, we'd carry ammunition up to the trenches after dark, after night.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I know I carried a crate of live bombs on my left shoulder. In the dark you had to go through these trenches, you know how they go. Crooked. And we made fun of us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One fella said, Now what happens if you drop those bombs?

speaker: John Eikum
words: I says we'd get a quick ticket to kingdom come. But they all joked about it. We were in reserve and the other battalions were up in the trenches.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's this story of the mythical army of Verdun?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well that was it. The mythical army of Verdun. They took us out after dark and marched us back. Back behind the lines. And they'd take us up again the next day. Or next night. That way we went on for a week. And the Germans thought there was new troops coming in all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They'd have you march back under cover of darkness? And have you march up?

speaker: John Eikum
words: We marched back under cover of darkness and we couldn't figure why in the world. We was up there one night. We was in the town one night. And marched up there. Next night they marched us right back to the same town. They kept that up and we couldn't figure out what in the world they were doing that for.

speaker: John Eikum
words: But they had a... that was the mythical army.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you find out about why they had done that?

speaker: John Eikum
words: After I got home here and read it in the paper. They had an article about the mythical army of Verdun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You didn't know until then that that's why you guys were marching back and forth?

speaker: John Eikum
words: We couldn't figure out. I didn't know until after I got here I read the story of the mythical army of Verdun. There was two battalions, two battalions of Americans. They were holding four German divisions.

speaker: John Eikum
words: You see, they would like to have sent relief up to Sedan. And they didn't let go because they figured if they moved them up there, we would break through there at Sedan and cut their rail line between Metz and Sedan. So they let their German intelligence listen in, you know, when they heard these new troops coming up every night, and the ditches that go in the same outfits.

speaker: John Eikum
words: They were kind of sneaky.

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, I laughed about that after I got home and read the story in the paper here about the mythical army of Verdun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was you?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, that was me.

speaker: John Eikum
words: We was in there for nine days. And you know when we come out, we were so weak after we hadn't had any exercise that we couldn't hardly stand up when we got out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Out of the underground?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Because you didn't have any water there. All the water you got was that canteen and that had to last you for nine days. You never washed your face or your hands for nine days.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And the 9th of the Armistice, we were moving down towards Metz. We were supposed to start the drive on Metz on the 13th of November.

speaker: John Eikum
words: That night we slept on the banks of the U .S. River. So there was two men to a pup tent. And this partner of mine that occupied my tent, we went down to the river bank and broke off a lot of cattails and put that underneath on the ground to keep some of the dampness off. Next morning we got up and we just saw the impression of our bodies right in the mud.

speaker: John Eikum
words: About 10 o 'clock when the Red Cross band came along, old Daddy Downer, he said, well boys, this is the war's over. It'll be over at 11 o 'clock.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And that's when hell broke loose.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Some of them started shooting up their guns, their rifles to celebrate, you know, and that was strictly forbidden.

speaker: John Eikum
words: But the first night I couldn't sleep because I'd been used to all that thunder, all roar all day and night, you know, and it got so still that I couldn't go to sleep. Everything got quiet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the story?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mentioned to me there was a general that wanted all the mines cleared out. Oh yes.

speaker: John Eikum
words: That was General Dugan. He was an Irishman, Dugan, but to sound a little better, he changed it to Dugan.

speaker: John Eikum
words: We come to a camp there, and whenever you came to a camp you had to clean it all up. He started to clean it up and one guy got his arm shot off. He picked up a grenade, you know, a live grenade. It was all covered in grenades and pieces of shell. So the sergeant said he quit. So the general came along after a little while and he said, I thought I gave orders to have this place thoroughly policed up. Well, the sergeant said, we got out here, started to police up, and the guy got his hand shot off. Well, the general said, this ground has to be thoroughly policed. He says, that meant cleaned up.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Even if it takes every man to my command, he says, this has to be cleaned up.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, he was pretty good though. We had Captain Ray, who was the captain of our company, and he put on, he bought himself a coat that went down here with a fur collar on it. He always kept his cap over on the side, you know. The general happened to meet him one day in the orderly room, or the room where it was nothing but a tent. So the general again got right up in front of the captain. He took his cap and put it on the side of his head, and hung it over his ear like the captain had his can.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now he said, wouldn't I be a hell of a looking soldier if I walked around with my cap like that all the time?

speaker: John Eikum
words: You know, the guy I hate was Captain Burnside.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And if he had been in command of the company when they went up to the front lines, he'd be the first man, he'd be the first casualty.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he like that made him so bad?

speaker: John Eikum
words: He's about six foot four, and was about 22 years old.

speaker: John Eikum
words: He'd ball you out for nothing.

speaker: John Eikum
words: He asked one, we said, line up out there one day, and he asked one of these.

speaker: John Eikum
words: It wasn't a Jap, we called him a WAP. We called him WAP, he was Greek.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He says, what's your serial number?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, the guy razzed up the number, he says, well, he says, you made a pretty good guess, you only missed it by about a million and a half.

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, we hated him.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And we hated him from the time we left the States.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He didn't know much about it really?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No. He said, he lined us up and was going on the boat.

speaker: John Eikum
words: He says, men, he says, I'll tell you one thing. Now, if there's any one of you that has a yellow streak down his back, you can step out, he says, and you won't have to go across. That made us mad.

speaker: John Eikum
words: There's only one guy stepped out of there, 250.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And another guy that wouldn't let go. They wouldn't send him across. Because he had the German vaccination mark, and if he ever had taken a prisoner, they'd take him out and shoot him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He had a German vaccination mark?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Yeah, he'd been in the, he had, he'd been vaccinated in Germany. His name was Prince.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, why would they shoot him? Because he was a German fighting for the Americans?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, he might be a German spy.

speaker: John Eikum
words: We even had a, we had our Sergeant Major down in Camp Lewis, we had him in detention.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Sergeant Major Ritter, he was a German. He'd been in the German Army. We had him in solitary confinement down here at Camp Lewis because there were some secret papers that had disappeared from the division headquarters down there. And he was in charge of that, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they thought he was guilty?

speaker: John Eikum
words: They thought he might be guilty of sending out those secret papers.

speaker: John Eikum
words: We took him out once a day to exercise.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know if he was ever cleared?

speaker: John Eikum
words: I don't know why. We left before he ever went to a hearing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mentioned there was one guy that really was afraid to fight.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Oh, that was going across. He was a big Swede, and he's afraid, he's afraid to have submarines or sink some of the ships. So he sat down at the lower step of the ladder that went up on deck every night. He never slept going across. It took us 14 days to cross. That's the first time I've seen a snowstorm in July. We run into a snowstorm because we went from

speaker: John Eikum
words: We went from New York or from Staten Island up along the coast until we hit Greenland. Then we went along the coast of Greenland until we got over there, and then we went down south again, got into the Irish Sea. That's the way we got into Liverpool.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he was afraid about fighting?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, he thought there was a submarine. He was afraid of the submarine. Oh, I thought you said he thought he was going to be killed. Well, that's what he's afraid of.

speaker: John Eikum
words: And he's the first man hurt.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about you? Were you afraid of dying in that war?

speaker: John Eikum
words: We didn't think about it.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Not even the night I was on guard.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Of course we didn't. We didn't face any small rifle fire. It was mostly mini -werfers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what they are?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No. Flying pigs. Little bombs with a propeller at the back of it. You could hear the big shells coming. You could hear the whine of a big shell long before they came there. But the mini -werfers, they'd just come up and they'd say, boof, and explode right away. That's what we called them. They'd just make a grunt when they'd come over, you know. That's why we called them flying pigs. No eyes in a predicament. One night, we got the gas alarm. And here I was trying to get on, put on my gas mask. And I wrapped leg and come unwrapped. I was dragging that and I was trying to get my mask on and get my legging back on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You managed it, huh?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Then I went down in a dugout. They had dugouts up there. I barely missed getting hit. I heard a piece of shrapnel go right past my back and hit the wall on the other side. It never scared me. I went right out again. We had to keep count of the shells that exploded that night. They threw over 2 ,500 shells.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Bombs is what they were. They were great big shells, about that long. And then they were loaded with all kinds of scrap iron. And when they exploded, that scrap iron would fly all over, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you think that it was a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of the experience of being over there?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, like the fellow said about his wife.

speaker: John Eikum
words: He wouldn't take a million for the experience and I wouldn't give a dime for another one just like it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's how you felt about being over there?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I tried to get her so she could go downtown looking for me.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Oh, we had a good time after the armistice.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I made a friend with an old Frenchman.

speaker: John Eikum
words: His name was Ira Sylvain. I had a little notebook and he put his autograph in there. He says his name was Ira Sylvain. In 55 years, he'd been around the American and English camps.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I made friends with him. I gave him a ten franc note one night. And he jumped up and down. He says, Pour moi, pour moi. He says, For me, for me. I says, Oui. Pour mouve. So next day, he invited me down to the wine house and he treated me to a glass of wine.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I had his name and address in that little notebook. But that was thrown away when we cleaned out everything and left the ranch. I left all my trophies up there too.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I had a 75 millimeter shell, empty shell, all stamped out, you know, with flowers. We had a guy there that stamped out. He just had a little, not a chisel, but a punch. A punch and a hammer is all he used. I've seen those decorated. They make real nice decorations. I had a clip of French ammunition that was left. And I had a bullet that the guards used. You see, the guards used dumb, dumb bullets. When they hit you, they'd explode inside you and just blow you wide open.

speaker: John Eikum
words: But I, the best thing I had got stolen from me on the boat coming across. I had a cigarette lighter made out of a empty bombshell. All decorated up, you know. You could flip it like you did the ordinary. It had a flint in there. It looked like a little book. It's about so white, I guess, and about so high. Shaped like mining on a Bible. And all decorated up with the punch marks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mr. I'm going to have to get going. I was supposed to see somebody at two and it's going on three. We've been talking a lot longer than that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who were you supposed to meet at three?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was supposed to see him at two. His name is Michigan Bill Stowell. I don't know if you know him at all. He's an old time lumberjack. I was going to see him at two and he might not be home now, but I was supposed to see him so I've got to go down. There was one more thing I wanted to ask you just to get your opinion on it. That was about the community there in Genesee, near Genesee where you grew up. The two churches. We talked about it more or less.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But do you think that having the two churches, do you think that having the two churches, when they combined them into one, did that bring people a lot closer together? Did that end the division?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That ended the division, of course.

speaker: John Eikum
words: They had to put up with it. Now in Genesee, that's combined with the Lutheran church in St. John's Lutheran in Genesee now. They have the same minister. They're called the same minister.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think though when they had the two churches that it stood in the way of people being friends with each other?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the people didn't pay much attention to it?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Only a few. A few of the higher ups. They never visited with any of the upper church. The nervous especially. They were pretty strict on their own church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about when you were young then, did you play with kids from the lower church as well as kids from the upper church? Or did you stick with kids from the upper church?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, mostly in the upper church. We'd get together once in a while.

speaker: John Eikum
words: See, I was pretty sweet on Palma Hobie. Oh. I thought she was the most beautiful girl in the community. She belonged to the lower church. She belonged to the Trinity. Of course, they didn't call it Trinity. In those days, the Norwegian name, Trivoldaheds, Minniihed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you spent a little time down there.

speaker: John Eikum
words: Well, I spent most of the time down at my uncle and aunt and they just lived right across 40 acres. Half across the 40 acres from Hanson. Her name was Palma Hanson.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I was too old and she was too old when I got ready to get married. I was 36 when I got married and my wife was just past 20. I dropped her several times. I said she was too darn old when I married her. She was 20 and I was 36. She was a neighbor girl. We lived only about half a mile apart.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In those days, were they very strict about courting? If you were dating a girl in those days, were you supposed to just go places like social gatherings?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: John Eikum
words: You hardly ever took a girl out by yourself. In the earliest days. But then, before I got married, that was in 25. The restrictions. I took Esther along on a trip up to the homestead. We took a trip down to St. Mary's and out along the north and south highway out towards Harrison and back. That's where I put my gut wrecked. We went to a dance. I danced all night and then started on that trip up to St. Mary's. That was 22 miles. We had lunch at St. Mary's and then we took a trip out towards Harrison and drove back to her place. Then I started from her place to come down here to the folks where I stayed and that was too much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where the upper spring is?

speaker: John Eikum
words: Up on top of the hill. Yeah, more than that.

speaker: John Eikum
words: I was about half asleep. I was sitting there about half asleep driving the old star. I just happened to feel that it was getting out in the gravel and I woke up with a star. Now, you're out over an edge. I was about half asleep driving the old star. Now, you're out over an edge. Then I stopped at the spring. They have a spring up there. I washed my face in cold water and when I got halfway down the hill I stopped again. They had a water trough there and I washed my face in cold water there again. So I made it. It was pretty tough going.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's about the way the Indians were treated on the reservation. You told me the Coeur d 'Alene's weren't treated very well in the time you were up there. No.

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, they weren't treated very well. That's about the episode there. I worked out to the Hague at Amida. That's about eight or nine miles from the homestead. The deputy assessor came over and he wanted my aunt to go to Hague. And the Indians there, the Indian woman, she come with a can or a bucket when they wanted some skim milk. She wanted, so she poured out a couple of gallons of skim milk. The old Indian wanted, me wanted more. Me wanted more. So the lady just took the milk in the Indian's bucket and put it right back in her milk can and let it go without any milk. Well, they hated Indians up there because they came out there on the flats east of Sanders, in western Amida. And dug canvas roots. You see the canvas growths all over that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They didn't like that?

speaker: John Eikum
words: No, they didn't want them on the fields. They hated the sight of an Indian, anyway. And then that Indian come to one of the boys. He had two boys and bought some hay. He wanted four shocks of hay. So the kid told him, well, it'll cost you twenty -five cents a shock. So when the Indian come next morning to get his hay, the old man was there. The old man Wilkes was there. He wouldn't let him have any hay. And the Indian says, last night he says, I'm buying hay. I buy one shock, twenty -five cents, two shocks, fifty cents, three shocks, seventy -five cents, and four shocks a dollar. He says, I want my hay, but he didn't get any hay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had he paid for it already? Had the Indian paid for it?

speaker: John Eikum
words: I don't think he had. If he had, he should have got the hay anyway. But he arranged to get it. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder why they hated him so much. Why they disliked the Indian so much. They're kind of a nuisance.

speaker: John Eikum
words: If you want to get them started, they come and demand everything, you know. But I had a talk with the old squaw. She's over eighty years old, old Spotty. She had one dark spot and one bright spot all over her face. She was all spotted. We called her old Spotty. She come one day and they had one car there and she was driving by an old gray team. And the vibration she went through.

speaker: John Eikum
words: She says, this was the whoosh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He come, he come?

speaker: John Eikum
words: I says, no, he won't come. She's afraid of the car, you see. She says, whoosh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He come, he come? Old gray horse?

speaker: John Eikum
words: The old gray horse was shot when the car came. I said, he won't come. He just made a trip once a day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There weren't many cars in that country. No, just one.

speaker: John Eikum
words: But I did a few. I told her anyhow that he wouldn't come. Then she asked me if I had any tamak. Tamako. She went and bought some tamak. I said, no. No tamak. And then I had a hard man. He was doing some shot to get his ways out. He tamak, he tamak. I said, no, he no tamak.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think the Indians up there were mistreated or do you think they deserved what they got?

speaker: John Eikum
words: I don't know. You know they weren't like the Nez Perce Indians. The Nez Perce Indians are the most advanced Indians of all the tribes.

speaker: John Eikum
words: They're pretty well educated. And you can see the difference.

speaker: John Eikum
words: The Coeur d'Alene Indians, they're smaller than the Nez Perce Indians. And they all look old before their age. But you take these Indians out here, they all look well fed. Especially one, he's dead now, types. Chilled types. He's a good friend of mine, but he weighed about 240 pounds. He's a great baseball player. He was a catcher. I see him send a drive out there to center field when he played against Tennessee. And the center feeder caught it just about that high off the ground, you know, and then he flipped over three or four times. I asked Phil about that after I met him down here in Lewiston. We played cards together down here in Lewiston. Well, Ty said that's the hardest ball I ever hit. He got cleared down to second base before he ever caught it. He said that's the hardest ball I ever hit. I don't see how that fella caught it.
